coredata.dc:title: Visible-light-induced strong oxidation capacity of metal-free carbon nanodots through photo-induced surface reduction for photocatalytic antibacterial and tumor therapy
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Huiqi
Cheng, Quansheng
Lei, Josh Haipeng
Hao, Tianwei
Deng, Chu-Xia
Tang, Zikang
Qu, Songnan
coredata.dc:description: Biocompatible metal-free carbon dots (CDs) with good photo-induced strong oxidation capacity in aqueous solutions are scarce for high-performance photocatalytic antibacterial and tumor therapy. In this work, we achieved effective visible light-induced cell death and antibacterial performance based on biocompatible metal-free CDs. The visible-light-induced reducing ability of the surface electron-withdrawing structure of the CDs allowed for the remaining photo-induced holes with high oxidation capacity to oxidize water molecules and generate hydroxyl radicals. Antibiotic-resistant bacteria were effectively inhibited by the CDs under xenon lamp irradiation with 450 nm long pass filter. Moreover, CD-based tumor photocatalytic therapy in mice was achieved using a xenon lamp with 450 nm long pass filter (0.3 W cm−2).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Photo induced oxidation capacity
Tumor photocatalytic therapy
Antibacterial
Visible light

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: H. Zhang, Q. Cheng and J. H. Lei contributed equally to this work. This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [No. 61922091 ], the Science and Technology Development Fund of Macau SAR [ 0073/2019/AMJ , 0128/2020/A3 , 0131/2020/A3 , 006/2022/ALC and 0007/2021/AKP ], Fund from the University of Macau [MYRG2020-00164-IAPME], the Research and Development Grant for Chair Professor Fund from the University of Macau [ CPG2020-00026-IAPME ], the Shenzhen-Hong Kong-Macao Science and Technology Innovation Project (Category C) [SGDX20210823103803021]. All animal experiments were conducted with the approval of the University of Macau Animal Ethics Committee, Protocol No. UMARE-015-2019 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of CDs. (b) Absorption spectra of 30 µg mL−1CD aqueous solution after 0 min, 5 min, 10 min, 15 min and 20 min of irradiation xenon lamp (0.3 W cm−2, 450 nm LP filter). The insets show the CD aqueous solution before (left) and after (right) 20 min of xenon lamp irradiation (0.3 W cm−2, 450 nm LP filter). (c) Emission spectra of CDs and ir-CDs in dilute aqueous solutions under 555 nm excitation. Inset is the luminescence decays of CDs and ir-CDs in aqueous solutions monitored at 638 nm under 510 nm excitation (IRF, instrument response function). (d) Electron paramagnetic resonance spectra of CDs (50 µg mL−1) with DMPO as the spin trap in the dark, after 10 min xenon lamp irradiation (0.3 W cm−2, 450 nm LP filter) with and without 2 mg mL−1 potassium iodide (KI).

(a) Fluorescent images of 4T1 cells incubated with CDs (250 µg mL−1) for 1 h at 560 nm excitation. (b) Cell viabilities of 4T1 cells treated with different concentrations CDs in the dark or with 0.3 W cm−2 xenon lamp irradiation using a 450 nm LP filter (5 min) with and without isopropyl alcohol (IPA) or KI for 24 h. Data represent mean ± standard deviation (n = 3). Live/dead viability assays performed for 4T1 cells treated with (c) no CDs, (d) CDs (250 µg mL−1), and (e) CDs (250 µg mL−1) with KI (1 mg mL−1) at 470 nm excitation after xenon lamp irradiation for 10 min (0.3 W cm−2, 450 nm LP filter).

(a) Full survey of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of CDs and ir-CDs. High-resolution (b) C 1 s, (c) N 1 s, and (d) O 1 s XPS spectra of CDs and ir-CDs deconvolved into several peaks and labeled with different colors.
(a) Possible surface structural changes of the CDs after visible light irradiation. (b) Schematic diagram of the possible photocatalytic processes of the CDs in aqueous solutions.

Photographs of tetracycline (TC)-resistant Escherichia coli on eosin methylene blue agar plates for 48 h under different conditions: (a) in the dark, (b) 250 µg mL−1 CDs, (c) 10 μg mL−1 TC, (d) xenon lamp irradiation for 10 min (0.6 W cm−2, 450 nm LP filter), and (e) 250 µg mL−1 CDs and xenon lamp irradiation for 10 min (0.6 W cm−2, 450 nm LP filter).(f) Corresponding colony forming unit statistics were normalized to the dark group. The experiments were conducted in triplicate and expressed as the means ± standard deviation.

Photographs of mice during the study period under different treatment conditions: (a) 100 μL 250 µg mL−1 CDs and xenon lamp irradiation for 10 min (0.3 W cm−2, 450 nm LP filter), (b) 100 μL 250 µg mL−1 CDs, and (c) xenon lamp irradiation for 10 min (0.3 W cm−2, 450 nm LP filter). (d) Tumor growth curves of 4T1 tumors in mice after different treatments (n = 5 for each group). The curves of each group were processed based on the tumor sizes (mean ± standard deviation). (e) Survival rates of mice after different treatments. (f) Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E), cleaved caspase 3 (c-Cas-3), and Ki67 staining of the tumor tissues on day 12 after treatment in mice. Scale bar: 100 µm.
Specific C, N, and O content for different chemical bonds in CDs and ir-CDs in the XPS spectra.

head.ce:title: Visible-light-induced strong oxidation capacity of metal-free carbon nanodots through photo-induced surface reduction for photocatalytic antibacterial and tumor therapy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Joint Key Laboratory of the Ministry of Education
Institute of Applied Physics and Materials Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High-performance photocatalytic antibacterial and tumor therapy in metal-free CDs under visible light was demonstrated by the photo-induced reduction of the surface electron-withdrawing groups, leading to an extended lifetime of the photo-generated holes with strong oxidation capacity, which oxidize water molecules to hydroxyl radicals in aqueous solution.

Biocompatible metal-free carbon dots (CDs) with good photo-induced strong oxidation capacity in aqueous solutions are scarce for high-performance photocatalytic antibacterial and tumor therapy. In this work, we achieved effective visible light-induced cell death and antibacterial performance based on biocompatible metal-free CDs. The visible-light-induced reducing ability of the surface electron-withdrawing structure of the CDs allowed for the remaining photo-induced holes with high oxidation capacity to oxidize water molecules and generate hydroxyl radicals. Antibiotic-resistant bacteria were effectively inhibited by the CDs under xenon lamp irradiation with 450 nm long pass filter. Moreover, CD-based tumor photocatalytic therapy in mice was achieved using a xenon lamp with 450 nm long pass filter (0.3 W cm−2).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Reactive oxygen species, including singlet oxygen (1O2), peroxide (O2 2−), superoxide radicals (·O2−), and hydroxyl radicals (·OH), are highly reactive chemicals that play an important role in cancer therapy and antibacterial applications. Compared with the other reactive oxygen species, hydroxyl radicals (·OH) have the highest oxidation capacity, which can cause more significant lipid peroxidation and oxidative DNA damage [1–4]. Photodynamic therapy (PDT), chemodynamic therapy (CDT), and photocatalytic therapy (PCT) can produce hydroxyl radicals. The generation of hydroxyl radicals in PDT is usually dependent on the content of oxygen molecules in the aqueous media, which limits its efficacy in tumors in a hypoxic microenvironment [5–9]. For CDT, the generation of hydroxyl radicals not only relies on transition metal catalysts but is also dependent on the H2O2 content in the tumor microenvironment [10]. PCT produces hydroxyl radicals based on photogenerated holes to oxidize hydroxide ions in water, which is not dependent on the oxygen and H2O2 content in the tumor microenvironment [9,11–16].

Until now, few photocatalytic nanomaterials have been reported for PCT. Wang et al. reported synergistic photothermal therapy (PTT)-CDT-PCT employing Cu2−xSe–Au Janus nanoparticles with an average size of 30 nm under 808 nm excitation. Photo-induced electron-hole separation occurred in Cu2−xSe, where the photogenerated holes introduced hydroxyl radicals from the hydroxide ions in aqueous solution [17]. Yang et al. reported a full solar-spectrum-driven antibacterial PCT based on the Sn3O4/PDINH heterostructure photocatalyst. The nanocrystal structure and molecular dipoles created by the built-in electric field contributed to the separation of photogenerated carriers. The Sn3O4/PDINH hybrid heterostructure provided enhanced photogenerated carrier separation and significantly inhibited the recombination of photo-induced electrons and holes [13]. The reported photocatalyst agents for PCT were based on nanocomposites, which usually contained heavy metal elements with potential biosafety risks. Thus, it is highly desired to develop biocompatible metal-free photocatalysts with good photo-induced hydroxyl radical generation in aqueous media.

Carbon dots (CDs) are an ideal class of carbon-based nanomaterials with simple preparation, broad absorption, good biocompatibility, and low cytotoxicity, providing distinct advantages in biological applications [18–20]. Pure CDs and CD complexes have been employed as photosensitizers and photocatalysts to generate hydroxyl radicals. For example, Zhao et al. reported CDs synthesized from 1,3,6-trinitropyrene using a hydrothermal method that can produce singlet oxygen, hydroxyl radicals, and heat under 635 nm laser irradiation for combined PDT and PTT [21]. Zhang et al. reported a CDs/Na2W4O13/WO3 composite that exhibited antimicrobial activity against Escherichia coli (E. coli) by generating hydroxyl and superoxide radicals under visible light irradiation. In this composite, the CDs inhibited the recombination of the photogenerated charge carriers in Na2W4O13 by trapping the photogenerated electrons [22]. However, CD-based PCT was reported only in CDs/semiconductor composite systems [23,24].

For pure CD systems to generate sufficient photo-induced hydroxyl radicals with strong oxidation capacity, the photo-excited electrons cannot undergo recombination with the photo-generated holes. The lower phototoxicity of visible-light-induced photocatalysis is highly desired for biological applications. To the best of our knowledge, there are no reports on pure metal-free CDs that can produce photo-generated holes with high oxidation capacity under visible light irradiation in aqueous media.

In our previous work, we reported pure red emissive CDs through surface dominant electron-withdrawing group functionalization derived from citric acid and urea in formic acid using a solvothermal method. The rich electron-withdrawing structure of the CD surface caused by the high content of CO groups contributed to its pure red emission [25]. In this work, we achieved a high visible-light-induced oxidation capacity using these CDs. The electron-withdrawing groups on the surface trapped photo-excited electrons and induced the reduction reaction, which prolonged the lifetime of the photo-generated holes, improving the oxidation capacity by oxidizing water molecules to hydroxyl radicals in aqueous solution. In the dark, the CDs exhibited low cytotoxicity. Upon visible light irradiation, the photo-generated holes from the CDs and their generated hydroxyl radicals can effectively kill cells and antibiotic-resistant bacteria. In vivo, the PCT of tumors was also achieved using CDs through xenon lamp irradiation with 450 nm long pass filter (0.3 W cm−2). It is the first time of demonstrating photocatalytic tumor therapy based on pure metal-free CDs.

Citric acid (AR, 99.5%), urea (≥99.5%) and potassium iodide (KI) (≥99%) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Formic acid (≥97%) and isopropanol (IPA) (≥99.8%) was purchased from Acros Organics. Sodium chloride (GR) and anhydrous ethanol (AR) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Works, and 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai) Trading Co., Ltd. Cell Counting Kit-8 was purchased from Beyotime Biotech. Inc. Tetracycline (TC) (≥98%) was purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd., eosin-methylene blue (EMB) agar was purchased from Shanghai Sincere Biotech Co. Ltd, peptone was purchased from Beijing Aoboxing Biology Technology Co., Ltd., and yeast extract was purchased from Guangdong Huankai Microbial Sci. & Tech. Co., Ltd. All reagents were required no further purification.

The CDs were prepared from citric acid (1 g) and urea (2 g) by solvothermal treatment in formic acid (10 mL) at 160 °C 4 h in a Wattcas autoclave (WP-MSAR-250A). The dark red-brown solutions obtained after solvothermal treatment were mixed with 20 mL of ethanol, shaken well, centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min, and repeated 2–3 times. The precipitates were freeze-dried to obtain the black product.

Transmission electron microscopy (FEI TecnaiG2F30; 200 kV) was used to investigate the morphology of the samples. Cell imaging and live/dead staining assay were performed using an IX73 inverted microscope (Olympus). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed with an ESCALAB 250Xi photoelectron spectrometer from Thermo Fisher Scientific, using Mo as the excitation source. The UV–vis absorption spectra were collected using a JASCO V-770 spectrophotometer. The photoluminescence spectra and photoluminescence lifetime were measured with an Edinburgh FS5 spectrophotometer. The electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy measurements were recorded on a Magnettech ESR5000 (Bruker). The visible light source was a xenon lamp (BBZM-III, cold light source) with a 450 nm long pass (LP) filter.

DMPO was used as the capture agent to detect •OH by EPR. The CDs (50 µg mL−1) with DMPO (0.09 mol L−1) were set up in capillary tubes, and the EPR was tested before and after xenon lamp irradiation for 10 min (0.3 W cm−2, 450 nm LP filter).

The 4T1 cells were seeded in 6-well plates. After incubation for 24 h, the 4 T1 cells were incubated with CDs (250 µg mL−1) for 1 h. The culture medium was removed and washed three times with phosphate buffered saline (PBS). The live cells were imaged using an Olympus IX73 inverted microscope with a 40x objective lens at 560 nm excitation with a 590 nm LP filter.

The CCK-8 assay was performed to assess the cytotoxicity of the CDs in the dark and under xenon lamp irradiation (0.3 W cm−2, 450 nm LP filter) with and without IPA or KI. 4T1 cells were seeded on a 96-well plate (5000 cells per well) and incubated overnight. Then, the medium was changed with 100 μL of fresh medium containing 0–250 µg mL−1 CDs or CDs with IPA (1 mg mL−1 or 2 mg mL−1) or KI (1 mg mL−1). After incubation for 1 h, the cells were irradiated with a xenon lamp (0.3 W cm−2, 450 nm LP filter) for 5 min or incubated in the dark. Then, after 24 h of incubation, the medium was changed with 100 μL fresh medium containing 10 μL of CCK-8, and the cells were incubated for another 3–4 h. The absorbance at 450 nm was recorded using a Varioskan LUX Multimode Microplate Reader (Thermo Fisher Scientific) and normalized to 4T1 cells treated in the dark. Each experiment had three wells tested in parallel.

The 4T1 cells were incubated without the CDs, with CDs (250 µg mL−1), and with CDs (250 µg mL−1) and KI (1 mg mL−1) for 1 h, irradiated with a xenon lamp (0.3 W cm−2, 450 nm LP filter) for 10 min, and finally stained with calcein acetoxymethyl ester and propidium iodide at 37 °C for 20 min. After washing three times with PBS solution, the cells were observed with a fluorescent microscope (Olympus IX73 Inverted Microscope) using a 20x objective lens at 470 nm excitation with a 500 nm LP filter.

Photocatalytic antibacterial experiments were performed on TC-resistant E. coli and Staphylococcus aureus. TC-resistant E. coli were grown in lysogeny broth (LB) liquid medium containing 10 μg mL−1 TC and Staphylococcus aureus were grown in LB liquid medium, incubated at 37 °C for 24 h with shaking, harvested by centrifugation at 5000 rpm min−1 for 5 min, washed with PBS, and suspended in PBS at a concentration dilution of 5 × 104 colony forming unit (CFU) mL−1. The bacteria were mixed with CDs (final concentration: 250 μg mL−1) and exposed to xenon lamp irradiation (0.6 W cm−2, 450 nm LP filter) for 10 min. The other four groups of TC-resistant E. coli were treated (a) in the dark, (b) with 250 µg mL−1 CDs, (c) with 10 μg mL−1 of TC, and (d) under 10 min xenon lamp irradiation (0.6 W cm−2, 450 nm LP filter), then applied onto EMB agar plates, and incubated for 48 h at 37 °C. And the other three groups of Staphylococcus aureus were treated (a) in the dark, (b) with 250 µg mL−1 CDs, (c) under 10 min xenon lamp irradiation (0.6 W cm−2, 450 nm LP filter), then applied onto LB agar plates, and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. Each group was tested in triplicate. The corresponding colony-forming unit statistics were normalized to the dark group.

The tumor-bearing mouse model was established by the subcutaneous injection of 4T1 cells (5 × 105 per mouse) into the upper dorsal region of female BALB/c mice. When the tumor volume reached ∼ 80 mm3, the mice were randomly divided into four groups (n = 5 per group): CDs + xenon lamp, CDs, xenon lamp, and PBS. The mice were anesthetized using isoflurane. The mice in the CDs + xenon lamp group were intratumorally injected with 100 μL of 250 μg mL−1 CDs and irradiated with a xenon lamp (0.3 W cm−2, 450 nm LP filter) for 10 min. The tumor volume was recorded every two days and calculated as follows: V = ((tumor length) × (tumor width)2)/2. On day 12, the tumors of all mice were surgically removed and fixed with formalin (10% v/v), followed by hematoxylin and eosin (H&E), cleaved caspase 3 (c-Cas-3), and Ki67 staining.

The CDs were prepared according to our previous work [25]. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) results exhibited that the CDs were uniformly dispersed with an average particle size of about 4.2 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the CDs showed well-resolved lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.24 nm, which was ascribed to the (1120) crystal plane of graphite [26]. X-Ray diffraction pattern of CDs exhibited a strong peak at 2θ = 26.77°, which is conferred as the (002) diffraction patterns of graphitic carbon (Figure S1). [27] As shown in Figure S2, the Raman spectrum of CDs exhibited two major bands at 1354 and 1573 cm−1, which can be attributed to d-band (sp3) and G-band (sp2), respectively.[28] The CDs exhibited weak red fluorescence in aqueous solution. Upon increasing the irradiation time with a 0.3 W cm−2 xenon lamp with a 450 nm LP filter, the main absorption band at 553 nm from the CDs gradually decreased as shown in Fig. 1b. The CDs after xenon lamp irradiation for 20 min (0.3 W cm−2, 450 nm LP filter) were termed ir-CDs. The red fluorescence band of the ir-CDs was significantly reduced compared to that of the CDs (Fig. 1c). The inset shows the luminescence decay curves of the CDs and ir-CDs monitored at 638 nm under excitation at 510 nm. The shortened average fluorescence lifetime of the red emission indicated the change in the red emission center of the ir-CDs. Clear EPR signals were observed in the CD aqueous solution under xenon lamp irradiation (0.3 W cm−2, 450 nm LP filter). After xenon lamp irradiation for 10 min, a well-characterized 4-line signal with an intensity of approximately 1:2:2:1, indicating the DMPO spin adduct with hydroxyl radical,was detected in the CD aqueous solution as shown in Fig. 1d [29–31]. KI was used as a sacrificial agent to deplete the photogenerated holes. After adding KI, no EPR signal was observed in the CDs following xenon lamp irradiation with 450 nm LP filter for 10 min. Additionally, the main absorption band of the CDs with KI was also decreased more significantly than that of CDs (Figure S3).

The cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of CDs have been studied in our previous work [25]. CDs have low cytotoxicity and could be excreted from the mice via liver and kidney in 48 h after intravenous injections, making it a promising candidate for biological applications. As shown in Fig. 2 a, 4T1 cells incubated with CDs exhibited red fluorescence under 560 nm excitation, indicating that the CDs had entered the cells for fluorescence imaging. The cytotoxicity of CDs in the dark and under xenon lamp irradiation (0.3 W cm−2, 450 nm LP filter) with and without IPA or KI was further investigated in 4T1 cells by CCK-8 assay, as shown in Fig. 2b. After incubation in the dark for 24 h, there was no significant difference between the control cells and cells treated with CDs at a concentration of 250 µg mL−1, indicating the low cytotoxicity of the CDs. In contrast, high cytotoxicity was observed under 0.3 W cm−2 xenon lamp irradiation with a 450 nm LP filter (5 min) at CD concentrations of up to 250 µg mL−1, indicating that the CDs had strong visible-light-induced cell killing ability. IPA and KI were the capturing agents for hydroxyl radicals and photogenerated holes, respectively. The cell viability at the CD concentration of 250 µg mL−1 gradually increased from 16% to 36% and 60% by adding 1 and 2 mg mL−1 of IPA, respectively, while significantly increasing to 78% when 1 mg mL−1 of KI was added. The photocatalytic process had generated sufficient holes with strong oxidation capacity in the CDs to oxidize water or hydroxide to generate hydroxyl radicals. By comparing the cell viability after depleting hydroxyl radicals and photogenerated holes by IPA and KI, respectively, it was shown that the photo-generated holes and their generated hydroxyl radicals both contributed to visible-light-induced cell killing capacity. The live/dead cell viability staining assay further demonstrated the visible-light-induced cell killing ability of the CDs. Under xenon lamp irradiation (0.3 W cm−2, 450 nm LP filter), 4T1 cells without CDs exhibited green fluorescence, indicating no cell death, while almost all 4T1 cells treated with CDs showed red fluorescence, indicating significant visible-light-induced cell death in the presence of the CDs (Fig. 2c–2e). In the presence of KI, the CD-treated 4T1 cells mostly exhibited green fluorescence after the same xenon lamp irradiation, indicating weak visible-light-induced cytotoxicity by the depletion of photo-generated holes in the CDs.

Based on these results, possible visible-light-induced surface structural changes in the CDs and related changes in the oxidation capacity of the CDs in aqueous solutions are proposed in Fig. 4 . The CD surface was abundant in electron-withdrawing groups such as CO and pyridinic N. Upon visible light excitation, photo-induced charge separation occurred in the CDs. Photogenerated electrons in the conduction band (CB) of the CDs were captured by the surface CO and pyridinic groups and underwent photo-induced reduction to –OH and pyrrolic groups, respectively. The photogenerated holes in the valence band (VB) of the CDs interacted with water and oxidize H2O to generate hydroxyl radicals. In the presence of KI, which depleted the photogenerated holes, the recombination of photo-excited reduction of electron-withdrawing groups on the CD surface was more favored. The introduction of KI under xenon lamp irradiation with 450 nm LP filter led to a faster reduction in the absorption band at 553 nm and caused the EPR signal in the CDs to disappear. These findings supported the potential application of CDs in PCT.

The photo-induced antibacterial capacity of the CDs was further investigated. TC-resistant E. coli with CDs were irradiated with a xenon lamp (0.6 W cm−2, 450 nm LP filter) for 10 min and then incubated in EMB agar plates for 48 h. Photographs of the colonies grown on EMB agar plates are shown in Fig. 5 e. Four conditions of TC-resistant E. coli were set up as controls: (1) in the dark, (2) in the dark with CDs, (3) in the dark with TC, and (4) under xenon lamp irradiation (0.6 W cm−2, 450 nm LP filter), as shown in Fig. 5a-5d. The experiments were conducted in triplicate, and the results of the remaining two experimental groups are shown in Figure S4. It can be seen that TC-resistant E. coli treated with CDs showed a significant reduction in the number of colonies after xenon lamp irradiation for 10 min (0.6 W cm−2, 450 nm LP filter), while the number of colonies of TC-resistant E. coli treated with TC remained almost unchanged. The corresponding CFU statistics normalized to the dark group are shown in Fig. 5f. The number of CFUs for TC-resistant E. coli treated with CDs after xenon lamp irradiation for 10 min (0.6 W cm−2, 450 nm LP filter) was only 24% of that without irradiation. We further investigated the CD-based photocatalytic antibacterial in Staphylococcus aureus. As shown in the Figure S5, it can be seen that in the presence of CDs, the growth of Staphylococcus aureus can be effectively inhibited under the xenon lamp irradiation for 10 min (0.6 W cm−2, 450 nm LP filter), indicating that the CDs exhibited good visible-light-induced antibacterial performance.

Encouraged by the excellent visible-light-induced oxidation capacity of the CDs, visible-light-induced PCT on tumors was carried out. The tumor-bearing mouse model was established by the subcutaneous implantation of 4T1 breast cancer cells. When the tumor volume reached ∼ 80 mm3, the mice were treated with an intratumoral injection of 100 μL of 250 μg mL−1 CDs and exposed to xenon lamp irradiation (0.3 W cm−2, 450 nm LP filter) for 10 min (CDs + xenon lamp group). The control group consisted of mice treated with CDs without irradiation (CDs group), mice subjected to xenon lamp irradiation (0.3 W cm−2, 450 nm LP filter) for 10 min (xenon lamp group), and mice given PBS (PBS group). Photographs of each group of mice are shown in Fig. 6 a-6c and Figure S6-S9. After treatment, the tumor sizes of mice were measured every 2 days. As shown in Fig. 6d, the tumor size of the control groups, i.e., the CDs, xenon, and PBS groups grew quickly, while the CDs + xenon lamp group exhibited significantly inhibited tumor growth, confirming the effectiveness of CD-based tumor PCT. To further assess the anti-tumor effect, histopathological and immunohistochemical analysis of the tumor tissues of mice on day 12 after the different treatments was performed. As can be seen in Fig. 6f and Figure S10, there was no obvious tumor tissue damage in the control groups. However, in the CDs + xenon lamp group, H&E, c-Cas-3, and Ki67 stained images showed significant cell damage and apoptosis with reduction of proliferative activity, confirming the effect of PCT on tumors. Moreover, the 30-day mouse survival rate increased from 0 to 100% after CD-based PCT. (Fig. 6e).

In conclusion, metal-free CD surfaces rich in electron-withdrawing groups exhibited a high visible-light-induced oxidation capacity, which can effectively achieve visible-light-induced cell death and antibacterial properties. By exploring the optical properties and chemical structure of the CDs before and after visible light irradiation, the electron-withdrawing groups on the CD surface can capture photo-excited electrons through photo-induced surface reduction. Thus, the lifetime of the photo-generated holes was prolonged, increasing the oxidation capacity to oxidize water molecules or hydroxyl ions to form hydroxyl radicals in aqueous solution. In the dark, the CDs exhibited low cytotoxicity. Under visible light irradiation, the photo-generated holes and hydroxyl radicals from the CDs were effective in killing cells and antibiotic-resistant bacteria. In vivo, the CD-based PCT of tumors under visible light irradiation was also demonstrated in mice models. To our knowledge, this is the first published investigation of photocatalytic tumor therapy based on pure metal-free CDs under visible light irradiation. In addition, this study can promote the development of CDs in the field of PCT.

Huiqi Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Quansheng Cheng: Validation, Investigation. Josh Haipeng Lei: Validation, Investigation. Tianwei Hao: Resources. Chu-Xia Deng: Resources. Zikang Tang: Resources, Funding acquisition. Songnan Qu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: H. Zhang, Q. Cheng and J. H. Lei contributed equally to this work. This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [No. 61922091], the Science and Technology Development Fund of Macau SAR [0073/2019/AMJ, 0128/2020/A3, 0131/2020/A3, 006/2022/ALC and 0007/2021/AKP], Fund from the University of Macau [MYRG2020-00164-IAPME], the Research and Development Grant for Chair Professor Fund from the University of Macau [CPG2020-00026-IAPME], the Shenzhen-Hong Kong-Macao Science and Technology Innovation Project (Category C) [SGDX20210823103803021]. All animal experiments were conducted with the approval of the University of Macau Animal Ethics Committee, Protocol No. UMARE-015-2019.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2023.04.032.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
